title: Using the A-Word, Indiana Abolitionist John Jacob Unseats Incumbent Pro-Life Rep.
author: James Silberman
orientation: Reports John Jacob's June 2020 Indiana primary win over pro-life incumbent Dollyne Sherman as the first time an avowed abolitionist unseated a pro-lifer, and argues that running openly with abolitionist language (not pro-life language) is electorally viable and strategically superior.
audiences: Pro-life voter weighing whether 'abolition' is a losing label
Movement strategist debating messaging and language
State legislator or aspiring statehouse challenger

questions.question: What's the headline result from Indiana State House District 93, and why is it being treated as a first?
questions.answer: Abolitionist John Jacob defeated pro-life incumbent Dollyne Sherman in the Republican primary, and the article frames it as the first time an abolitionist successfully unseated a sitting pro-life officeholder rather than merely converting one already in office.
questions.quote: Last week, abolitionist John Jacob defeated pro-life incumbent Dollyne Sherman in the Republican primary race for Indiana State House District 93. There are a number of sitting State Senators and Representatives in various states who have converted to abolitionism, but Jacob's victory marks the first time an abolitionist has successfully unseated a pro-lifer.

questions.question: How explicit was Jacob about being an abolitionist rather than just another pro-life candidate?
questions.audience: Pro-life voter weighing whether 'abolition' is a losing label
questions.answer: Jacob led with the word 'abolish' on his Facebook header and campaign mailer, and the term 'pro-life' did not appear in his original campaign content at all except in one shared third-party endorsement post.
questions.quote: Nowhere on Jacob's website or Facebook page does the term "pro-life" appear except for one endorsement post shared by Jacob in which an organization calls him "pro-life." The term does not appear in the Jacob campaign's original content or materials, while the a-word is featured prominently both at the top of his Facebook page and on his abortion-related campaign mailer.

questions.question: Which organizations endorsed each side, and what does that lineup signal?
questions.audience: Movement strategist debating messaging and language
questions.answer: Jacob was backed by Abolish Abortion Indiana and Hoosiers for Life, while the establishment pro-life group Indiana Right to Life backed Sherman, showing a clean split between abolitionist groups and the pro-life establishment.
questions.quote: Jacob was endorsed by Abolish Abortion Indiana and Hoosiers for Life. Sherman was endorsed by Indiana Right to Life.

questions.question: What policy commitment did Jacob make tying him to the most controversial abolition bill in Indiana?
questions.audience: State legislator or aspiring statehouse challenger
questions.answer: He pledged to co-author Rep. Curt Nisly's Protection at Conception bill, the same bill that had drawn the ire of the Indiana pro-life establishment, instead of distancing himself from it for political safety.
questions.quote: If elected as State Representative, John will co-author with Representative Curt Nisly's Protection at Conception bill to protect all life from conception to natural death; including the support for and passing of this critical bill.

questions.question: What does the Protection at Conception Act actually do, in plain terms?
questions.audience: State legislator or aspiring statehouse challenger
questions.answer: It is a total and immediate abortion abolition bill that also nullifies any conflicting federal law, treaty, or order within Indiana that fails to protect the right to life.
questions.quote: The Protection at Conception Act is a bill that totally and immediately abolishes abortion and establishes that "any act, law, treaty, order, rule, or regulation of the United States government that fails to protect a person's unalienable right to life is null, void, and unenforceable in Indiana."

questions.question: How does Jacob's result compare to other abolition-friendly Indiana primary challengers?
questions.audience: Movement strategist debating messaging and language
questions.answer: Three other Indiana challengers supportive of the same bill ran with predominantly pro-life branding and lost; only Jacob, who led with the a-word, won.
questions.quote: There were three other primary challengers in Indiana with connections to the Abolitionist Movement who were supportive of the Indiana Protection at Conception Act. Two of these three ran explicitly as "pro-life" candidates and did not use the a-word at all. The third included the a-word a few times, but used "pro-life" more often. Jacob was the only winner.

questions.question: Does Silberman claim using the a-word is the reason Jacob won?
questions.audience: Movement strategist debating messaging and language
questions.answer: No. He explicitly disclaims a single-cause explanation and instead uses the result to refute the narrower claim that abolitionists categorically can't beat pro-life incumbents by running on an openly abolitionist platform.
questions.quote: But it is to say conclusively that the idea that primary challengers can't unseat pro-life incumbents by running on an explicitly abolitionist platform needs to be put to rest.

questions.question: Even when an abolitionist candidate loses, what upside does the article say running openly buys you?
questions.answer: It produces an educational dividend by exposing huge audiences to abolitionism as distinct from pro-lifeism, generating new converts to the movement long after the votes are counted.
questions.quote: Dan Fisher did not win the race, but by running on a loud-and-proud abolitionist platform, he exposed hundreds of thousands of people to the ideology of abolitionism as distinct from pro-lifeism which has born fruit in the form of many converts to the movement.

questions.question: How does Silberman answer the worry that using 'abolish' invites establishment smear campaigns?
questions.answer: He argues the smears are unavoidable for any serious abolitionist candidate, and that softening the language only forfeits the campaign's strongest contrast with the pro-life opponent.
questions.quote: This is a possibility, but that possible ire is unavoidable for the abolitionist candidate regardless of the language used unless the candidate completely hides the fact that they want to immediately abolish abortion. In that case, the abolitionist challenger loses one their primary lines of attack on their pro-life opponent – the fact that they're serious about abolishing abortion and their opponent isn't.

questions.question: What's the underlying theological framing Jacob uses on his campaign site?
questions.answer: He grounds the right to life in the imago Dei and divine authorship of life, treating abortion abolition as a creational and constitutional duty rather than a policy preference.
questions.quote: God is the Author and Creator of all life. God created all human beings in His image. Every human life is a gift from the Creator. It is an irrefutable scientific fact that human life begins at conception.

questions.question: What is the rhetorical case for the word 'abolish' itself?
questions.answer: Silberman closes by arguing the word communicates exactly the intended action, attaches to a biblically defensible ideology, and belongs on a banner rather than in a closet.
questions.quote: "Abolish" is a word that communicates boldly and precisely what one intends to do about abortion. It is associated with an ideology that is Biblically unimpeachable and a movement that is growing exponentially. It is nothing to be ashamed of. It is a banner to be held high.

related.title: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
related.reason: Backstory on the Nisly bill Jacob pledged to co-author and the establishment opposition that made his stance politically risky.

related.title: John Jacob Wins!
related.reason: The general-election follow-up to this primary victory, completing Jacob's path to the Indiana State House.

related.title: Reps. Curt Nisly, John Jacob Author the Bill to Abolish Abortion in Indiana
related.reason: Direct payoff of Jacob's campaign promise: he co-authors the Protection at Conception Act once seated.

related.title: Alaska Abolition Bill Author David Eastman Wins Primary
related.reason: Parallel case study of an explicitly abolitionist legislator winning a contested primary, useful for the broader 'abolitionists can win' thesis.

related.title: 5 Principles For Abolitionist Candidates in Republican Primaries
related.reason: Generalizes Silberman's tactical lessons from races like Jacob's into a playbook for future challengers.

related.title: Disgraceful! Pro-Life Establishment Lies About Abolitionist Candidate Warren Hamilton
related.reason: Concrete illustration of the establishment-smear scenario the article anticipates and tells abolitionists to expect.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: The ideological case for the linguistic and moral distinction Jacob's campaign weaponized.
